ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a lamb leg food product class; roasted preparation is not separately coded, so closest_parent is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked sheep meat broadly; a specific roasted lamb leg sub-code does not exist in the public hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227197003 identifies lamb as a substance; a roasted lamb leg preparation code is not present in the public release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of roasted lamb leg is approximately 500 g of bone-in roasted meat, intended as a shared dish for 3–5 people.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, leg, whole (shank and sirloin), separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4" fat, choice, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #17082
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for roasted lamb leg (lean and fat, choice grade) used as the primary nutrient anchor. The Chinese restaurant preparation adds cumin, chilli, garlic, soy sauce, and sesame oil marinade; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical Chinese-style seasoning. All other macros and micronutrients are taken directly from the USDA roasted lamb leg entry per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat products contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (carbohydrate_g = 0.0 per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature consistently indicate no measurable postprandial glucose response for pure meat foods. As per Veritas v2 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero); gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g portion of Chinese-style roasted lamb leg contributes approximately 760 mg sodium, representing about one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and reduced-sodium marinades are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted lamb leg contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of effectively zero, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments and in portions that respect total energy targets.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 100 g of roasted lamb leg provides approximately 4.5 g saturated fat and 89 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100–150 g and choose leaner cuts to stay within AHA saturated fat guidelines of less than 13 g per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Lamb Leg (富贵烤羊腿, Kǎo Yáng Tuǐ) is a whole lamb leg that has been marinated in a blend of cumin, chilli, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil, then slow-roasted at high heat until the exterior is deeply caramelised and the interior remains juicy and tender. The dish is a signature of Northern Chinese and Xinjiang culinary tradition, where lamb is the dominant protein and cumin-forward spicing is characteristic. In Singapore, it is served as a premium shared dish at Chinese restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant, typically priced around SGD 99 for a whole leg intended for 3–5 diners. The flavour profile is bold, smoky, and aromatic with a slight heat from chilli and a savoury depth from soy. Nutritionally, roasted lamb leg is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with negligible carbohydrate content, making it glycemically inert. It is a rich source of zinc, vitamin B12, iron, selenium, and niacin. The saturated fat and sodium content from seasoning warrant moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free in its base form but may contain soy and sesame allergens from the marinade.
common_user_queries: How many calories in roasted lamb leg?
Is lamb leg high in protein?
Bei Jiang Restaurant lamb leg calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy roasted lamb leg (exact preparation match for roasting method and cut), with sodium adjusted upward to reflect Chinese-style marinade seasoning. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for this dish. GI is assigned as 1 based on zero carbohydrate content, consistent with peer-reviewed GI literature for pure meat foods and Veritas v2 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods.
notes: carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — USDA FoodData Central reports 0 g carbohydrate for roasted lamb leg (pure meat, no breading or starchy coating). sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no added sugars in the meat itself; trace sugars from marinade are negligible and not separately reported. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero — meat contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source for this cut. glycemic_index is 1: set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = 0.0. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #17082 (roasted lamb leg). Sodium is adjusted from the USDA baseline (~65 mg/100 g for unseasoned roasted lamb) to approximately 380 mg/100 g to reflect typical Chinese restaurant marinade with soy sauce and salt; this is an estimation and should be verified against laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. The input cuisine_tags included 'Noodle' which appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded. The dish name '富贵烤羊腿' (Fùguì Kǎo Yáng Tuǐ) translates as 'Prosperous/Noble Roasted Lamb Leg', with 富贵 being a restaurant-specific branding prefix; the canonical Chinese name is 烤羊腿. Iteration 3 fix: corrected mixed-script error in aliases_by_language hindi entry — previous draft had 'भुनी हुई मेमne की टांग' (Latin 'ne' embedded in Devanagari) which did not match the pure Devanagari flat alias 'भुनी हुई मेमने की टांग'; both are now uniformly pure Devanagari and identical character-for-character.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Canonical food_name set to 'Roasted Lamb Leg'; restaurant branding prefix '富贵' moved to aliases. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy roasted lamb leg entry. Sodium adjusted upward from USDA baseline to reflect Chinese-style marinade; adaptation documented. Zero values for carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae documented with confirmed zero or naturally zero justification phrases. GI set to 1 with gi_category='low' and full documentation. Multilingual names added for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi. Ontology match_type values restricted to permitted set.
Iteration 2: aliases_by_language and flat aliases cross-checked for consistency. All romanized forms added to both structures.

Iteration 3: Corrected mixed-script error in aliases_by_language.hindi[0] — changed 'भुनी हुई मेमne की टांग' (containing Latin characters) to pure Devanagari 'भुनी हुई मेमने की टांग', matching the flat aliases entry exactly. Full character-for-character consistency audit of all aliases_by_language entries against flat aliases confirmed clean.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
